finding: Two separate sessions on the same day (Apr 14) used identical prompts to group uncommitted dotfiles changes into logical commits. The task is purely mechanical -- scan for changes, cluster by purpose, write conventional commit messages -- and recurs every time the user tweaks shell config, Ghostty, or Claude settings. A cron-triggered script (or a repo-autosync agent) could periodically auto-commit dotfiles with intelligent grouping, eliminating the manual session entirely.
existing_mitigation: The repo-autosync skill exists but is not wired to dotfiles. No cron or hook currently auto-commits this repo.

finding: On Apr 14 the user ran a full drift-analysis session: a structured prompt fed gsettings dumps, dconf exports, and package lists from live Mint into Claude to compare against the NixOS config repo. The goal is to catch customizations that would be lost on a fresh NixOS install. This task recurs every time the user tweaks Mint settings (DE switch to Cinnamon, redshift, keybindings, etc.). A scheduled drift-detection script could dump the live state, diff it against the NixOS repo, and surface only the deltas -- turning a multi-hour manual session into a periodic report.
existing_mitigation: None found. The nixos-config repo exists but has no automated drift detection.

finding: On Apr 14 the user discovered their disk was 'super packed' and had to manually investigate what was consuming space (pip cache, stale venvs, ISOs in Downloads, large repo directories). The investigation was reactive -- the sluggishness had already set in. A lightweight cron job checking disk usage and alerting (via TickTick or notification) when a threshold is crossed would catch this earlier and could auto-clean known safe targets like pip cache.
existing_mitigation: None found. No disk monitoring or alerting is configured.

finding: A 31-message session on Apr 14 was spent configuring notification sounds on both Mint and NixOS -- auditioning sounds, adjusting volume, and applying the choice to both systems. The user explicitly scoped the request as 'on both mint and nix', showing this is a cross-system concern. When the NixOS config matures to be the single source of truth (the stated migration goal), config changes like this would only need to be declared once. In the interim, a shared dotfile or script that applies the same sound config to both systems would halve the manual work.
existing_mitigation: The nixos-config repo and dotfiles repo exist but notification sound config is not yet in either.

finding: On Apr 14 the user asked 'Is Autodoro running? I think I've been here for a long while and I have not gotten any message.' The tool had silently stopped functioning. Additionally, the user had to manually request excluding whisper-writer from Autodoro's tracking. As the user builds more personal automation (autodoro, date-check cron, RSI cron, repo-autosync), a lightweight health-check dashboard or periodic 'are my automations alive' script would surface failures before the user notices the absence of expected behavior.
existing_mitigation: Autodoro is a systemd user service, so systemctl status could be checked. No health-check aggregator or alerting exists across the user's personal tools.
